food_subgroup: Beef braised in sweet soy sauce (kecap manis) with nutmeg and cloves

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Semur Daging. Closest parent is a sweet soy-braised beef stew concept. No exact URI available; gap documented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef braised in sweet soy sauce with spices (Indonesian semur daging style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0MEB used as closest parent for braised beef with sauce; no exact Semur Daging code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Semur Daging or Indonesian sweet soy-braised beef as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Semur Daging is approximately 200g (one bowl), comprising braised beef cubes and the dark sweet soy sauce, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Semur Daging — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional food composition table for Semur Daging. This is the highest-priority approved regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. Values represent a typical Javanese/Betawi home-cooked preparation with standard kecap manis proportion. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not reported in this source; set to -1.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Sweet soy sauce (kecap manis) palm sugar-based carbohydrate GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 (low) estimated via University of Sydney GI Database using the primary carbohydrate driver of Semur Daging — kecap manis, which is sweetened with palm sugar. Palm sugar has a GI of approximately 35–54 (low range) versus refined sucrose (GI ~65). The overall dish GI of 52 reflects the palm sugar-based sweetener diluted across the full dish matrix including beef protein and fat, which further blunts glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 200g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 17.0) / 100 = 8.84, rounded to 8.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from kecap manis (sweet soy sauce base)
hypertension.guidance: A 200g serving of Semur Daging delivers approximately 1,040mg sodium (43% of the 2,400mg daily limit); patients with hypertension should limit consumption to once per week and request reduced kecap manis in preparation, noting that significant reduction alters the dish's characteristic flavour.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content from kecap manis (palm sugar-based); low GI of 52
type2_diabetes.guidance: Semur Daging has a low GI of 52 driven by palm sugar in kecap manis, which has a lower glycaemic impact than refined cane sugar; a 200g serving delivers 13g sugar and a glycaemic load of 8.8, making moderate portion consumption acceptable for most Type 2 Diabetes patients when paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.8g/100g) and elevated sodium (520mg/100g); beneficial zinc and low trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Semur Daging contains 2.8g saturated fat and 520mg sodium per 100g — lower in saturated fat than coconut milk-based curries but sodium remains a concern; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150g, choose lean beef cuts, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (cloves/eugenol, nutmeg) and high zinc from beef
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The cloves (eugenol) and nutmeg in Semur Daging have documented anti-inflammatory properties, and the high zinc content (3.5mg/100g) from beef supports immune modulation; saturated fat is lower than coconut milk curries, making this a relatively suitable choice for rheumatoid arthritis patients when consumed in moderate frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Semur Daging is an Indonesian slow-braised beef dish with distinctive Dutch colonial influence — the word 'semur' derives from the Dutch 'smoor' (meaning to braise or stew). Beef cubes, typically from chuck or shin, are braised low and slow in kecap manis (Indonesian palm sugar-sweetened soy sauce) together with warming whole spices including nutmeg, cloves, and black pepper, finished with crispy fried shallots. The kecap manis gives the dish its characteristic dark, glossy, sweet-savory sauce with deep umami and a mildly caramelised sweetness. Semur Daging is a common Javanese home-cooking staple and a festive Betawi (Jakarta) dish, frequently served during Hari Raya celebrations. Nutritionally, a 100g serving provides 165 kcal, 15.5g protein, 7.5g fat (2.8g saturated), and 8.5g carbohydrate with 6.5g sugar from the kecap manis. Sodium is elevated at 520mg/100g due to the soy sauce base, making portion control important for hypertensive patients. The GI is low at approximately 52, driven by palm sugar in kecap manis which has a lower glycaemic impact than refined cane sugar. Beef contributes meaningful zinc (3.5mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (1.5mcg/100g). The spice base — cloves (eugenol) and nutmeg — provides anti-inflammatory phytochemicals. Semur Daging is distinguished from Chinese red-braised beef by the use of kecap manis rather than regular soy sauce, making it considerably sweeter and with a different flavour profile. It is halal and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: is semur daging good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional food composition table, which is an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. The GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database using the palm sugar carbohydrate driver of kecap manis rather than a direct Semur Daging GI measurement. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the available source. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the GI estimation basis and the absence of a direct exact-match entry for Semur Daging in the FAO/INFOODS table.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Semur Daging not available in the FAO/INFOODS SEA regional food composition table as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish. All three fields (-1) are documented here per G3 requirements. No core macros are zero; all macros are positive and sourced. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using palm sugar (kecap manis carbohydrate driver) as the reference food — this is the closest defensible approved GI source available. FoodOn URI is null as no exact FoodOn concept exists for Semur Daging; closest parent is sweet soy-braised beef stew. FoodEx2 code A0MEB used as closest parent. SNOMED CT has no applicable concept. Input source cited 'FAO/INFOODS SEA Table' which is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
